question: Explain the primary purpose of disk scheduling in an operating system.
expectedPoints: To improve the total throughput of the disk by reducing the total seek time.
To manage the order of I/O requests to minimize the movement of the disk arm.
To ensure fairness in request servicing and avoid starvation.

question: Describe how the SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) disk scheduling algorithm works and discuss one major drawback of this approach.
expectedPoints: SSTF selects the request that requires the least seek time from the current head position, minimizing the distance the head must move.
This approach is more efficient than FCFS (First-Come, First-Served) but is not optimal.
A major drawback is the potential for starvation of requests, as requests farther away from the current head position may be serviced very late or not at all if new requests keep arriving closer.

question: Compare and contrast the C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) and LOOK disk scheduling algorithms. Explain the operational difference between them and the performance benefit each aims to provide over a simpler algorithm like SCAN.
expectedPoints: C-SCAN moves the disk arm from one end of the disk to the other, servicing requests along the way. Upon reaching the end, it immediately returns to the beginning without servicing requests, creating a circular motion.
The LOOK algorithm is a variant of SCAN where the arm only goes as far as the last request in each direction before reversing, rather than moving to the absolute end of the disk.
C-SCAN provides a more uniform wait time for requests, as it treats the cylinders as a circular list. LOOK reduces the unnecessary traversal to the end of the disk, decreasing seek time.
Both aim to provide higher throughput and more fairness than SSTF by eliminating the risk of starvation for distant requests.
